channel.title: dotnet/docs Issues
channel.description: Issues from https://github.com/dotnet/docs, generated by https://github.com/meziantou/IssuesToRss/

item.title: Issue: Please elaborate syntax - @LukeOwlclaw
item.description:
The documentation shows how to correctly suppress a warning for a single member in a class, e.g. for F in following code it would be Scope = "member", Target = "~F:N.C.F":

namespace N
{
    class C
    {
        public int F;
        public int G;
    }
    class D
    {
        public int H;
        public int I;
    }
}

But what do we need need to write if we want to suppress all warnings in whole N? (Legacy format which causes IDE0077 would be Scope = "namespaceanddescendants", Target = "N")

How to suppress all warnings in class D?

Please note that also the documentation about "Target" does not explain the syntax and also StackOverflow has no answer.

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: e02c18e2-a05a-a2f3-adcd-1ee8bfeb09c5
  • Version Independent ID: f2cfe625-100a-bfb4-35c6-3ae0e52aab15
  • Content: IDE0077: Avoid legacy format target in global 'SuppressMessageAttribute' - .NET
  • Content Source: docs/fundamentals/code-analysis/style-rules/ide0077.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @gewarren
  • Microsoft Alias: gewarren

item.title: Issue: [Breaking change]: System.Drawing.Common no longer supported on non-Windows OSs - @safern
item.description:
Description

As proposed on: https://github.com/dotnet/designs/pull/234

System.Drawing.Common is now attributed as a Windows specific library which will cause the platform analyzer to emit warnings when compiled for non-windows OSs.

At runtime on non-windows OSs will now throw by default a TypeInitializerException with a PlatformNotSupportedException as the inner exception, unless a runtime config switch is set. The exception looks like this one:

System.TypeInitializationException : The type initializer for 'Gdip' threw an exception.
      ---- System.PlatformNotSupportedException : System.Drawing.Common is not supported on non-Windows platforms. See https://aka.ms/systemdrawingnonwindows for more information.
      Stack Trace:
           at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip.GdipCreateBitmapFromFile(String filename, IntPtr& bitmap)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Bitmap.cs(42,0): at System.Drawing.Bitmap..ctor(String filename, Boolean useIcm)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Bitmap.cs(25,0): at System.Drawing.Bitmap..ctor(String filename)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Resources.ResourceManager/tests/ResourceManagerTests.cs(270,0): at System.Resources.Tests.ResourceManagerTests.EnglishImageResourceData()+MoveNext()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Linq/src/System/Linq/Select.cs(136,0): at System.Linq.Enumerable.SelectEnumerableIterator`2.MoveNext()
        ----- Inner Stack Trace -----
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/LibraryResolver.cs(31,0): at System.Drawing.LibraryResolver.EnsureRegistered()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/GdiplusNative.Unix.cs(65,0): at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip.PlatformInitialize()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Gdiplus.cs(27,0): at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip..cctor()

To enable Unix support, the runtime config switch, System.Drawing.EnableUnixSupport, needs to be set to true.

Even if applications set the runtime config switch to true, this is just to give applications that heavily depend on this behavior cross plat, to migrate to more modern like libraries, and we will no longer give support for non-Windows bugs. This runtime config switch will be removed in .NET 7.

The libraries that we recommend libraries to search as an alternative are:

  • ImageSharp
  • SkiaSharp
  • Microsoft.Maui.Graphics

Version

.NET 6 Preview 7

Previous behavior

There was no compile warnings.

There was no runtime exception and no need to set a runtime config switch.

New behavior

  1. Compile time warnings emitted by the Platform analyzer as the assembly is now marked as windows specific.
  2. Runtime exception like the following unless a runtime config switch is set:
System.TypeInitializationException : The type initializer for 'Gdip' threw an exception.
      ---- System.PlatformNotSupportedException : System.Drawing.Common is not supported on non-Windows platforms. See https://aka.ms/systemdrawingnonwindows for more information.
      Stack Trace:
           at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip.GdipCreateBitmapFromFile(String filename, IntPtr& bitmap)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Bitmap.cs(42,0): at System.Drawing.Bitmap..ctor(String filename, Boolean useIcm)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Bitmap.cs(25,0): at System.Drawing.Bitmap..ctor(String filename)
        /_/src/libraries/System.Resources.ResourceManager/tests/ResourceManagerTests.cs(270,0): at System.Resources.Tests.ResourceManagerTests.EnglishImageResourceData()+MoveNext()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Linq/src/System/Linq/Select.cs(136,0): at System.Linq.Enumerable.SelectEnumerableIterator`2.MoveNext()
        ----- Inner Stack Trace -----
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/LibraryResolver.cs(31,0): at System.Drawing.LibraryResolver.EnsureRegistered()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/GdiplusNative.Unix.cs(65,0): at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip.PlatformInitialize()
        /_/src/libraries/System.Drawing.Common/src/System/Drawing/Gdiplus.cs(27,0): at System.Drawing.SafeNativeMethods.Gdip..cctor()

Type of breaking change

  • [ ] Binary incompatible (code must be recompiled to use the newer API version)
  • [ ] Source incompatible (code must be changed and recompiled to use the newer API version)

Reason for change

The cross-platform implementation of System.Drawing.Common is mainly provided on the native side, via libgdiplus. It's effectively a re-implementation of the parts of Windows that System.Drawing.Common is depending on. As you can imagine, that makes libgdiplus a very non-trivial component. It's around 30k lines of pretty old C code, virtually untested, and lacks a lot of functionality. libgdiplus also has a lot of external dependencies for all the image processing and text rendering, such as cairo, pango, and other native libraries, which makes maintaining and shipping it even more challenging.

Since System.Drawing.Common was really designed to be a thin wrapper over Windows technologies, its cross-platform implementation is a bit like a square peg in a round hole; it's subpar because it was only designed with Windows in mind.

Since the inclusion of the Mono cross-platform implementation we spent lot of time redirecting issues to libgdiplus that never got fixed, or helping people install libgdiplus correctly (because it's not part of .NET Core proper). This is very different from other external dependencies we have taken, for example, icu or openssl, which are high-quality libraries, rather than poorly maintained re-implementation of Windows components. Thus, we have come to believe that it's not viable to get libgdiplus to the point where its feature set and quality is on par with the rest of the .NET stack.

We have noticed (such as from analysis of NuGet packages) that System.Drawing.Common is used on cross-platform mostly for image manipulation like QR code generators and text rendering. We haven't noticed heavy graphics usage as our cross-platform graphics support is very incomplete. For example, right now some components crash on macOS because the native APIs we use have been deprecated and are no longer included with recent macOS releases. We have never gotten a report about that crash; we discovered it ourselves while making System.Drawing.Common trim compatible.

The usages we see of System.Drawing.Common in non-Windows environments are typically well supported with SkiaSharp and ImageSharp.

Thus, we believe it is not worth investing in making System.Drawing.Common work on non-Windows platforms and instead propose we only keep evolving it in the context of Windows Forms and GDI+.

Recommended action

Migrate code to one of the previously recommended libraries.

TODO: add examples.

Feature area

Other (please put exact area in description textbox)

Affected APIs

Namespace: System.Drawing

  • Bitmap
  • Image
  • Icon
  • Graphics
  • Brush
  • Brushes
  • Pen
  • Pens
  • BufferedGraphics
  • BufferedGraphicsContext
  • Font
  • FontFamily
  • FontConverter
  • IconConverter
  • ImageAnimator
  • Region
  • SolidBrush
  • StringFormat
  • SystemBrushes
  • SystemFonts
  • SystemIcons
  • SystemPens
  • TextureBrush

Namespace: System.Drawing.Design

  • AdjustableArrowCap
  • CustomLineCap
  • GraphicsPath
  • GraphicsPathIterator
  • GraphicsState
  • HatchBrush
  • LinearGradientBrush
  • Matrix
  • PathGradientBrush

Namespace: System.Drawing.Imaging

  • Encoder
  • EncoderParameter
  • EncoderParameters
  • ImageAttributes
  • ImageCodecInfo
  • ImageFormat
  • Metafile
  • MetafileHeader
  • PlayRecordCallback

Namespace: System.Drawing.Printing

  • PageSettings
  • PreviewPageInfo
  • PrintController
  • PrintDocument
  • PrinterSettings
  • PrintEventArgs
  • PrintPageEventArgs
  • PrintEventHandler
  • PrintPageEventHandler

Namespace: System.Drawing.Text

  • FontCollection
  • InstalledFontCollection
  • PrivateFontCollection

cc: @ericstj @terrajobst @danmoseley

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Pre-Migration --> Create a porting plan --> (notfinished) - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

The existing page... https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/porting/porting-approaches

Needs to be completely overhauled, it should be scenario based, that it is...

  • Address key top level scenarios such as...
    • Must continue to support .NET Fx for foreseeable future
    • Only need to support .NET Fx during migration
  • Address additional scenarios such as...
    • IIS to Kestrel
    • Moving to containers such as AKS
    • Becoming completely cross-plat

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Pre-Migration --> Create a porting plan - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

The existing pages...

  • https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/porting/porting-approaches
  • https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/porting/project-structure

Should be moved up under Pre-Migration

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Pre-Migration --> Needed changes before porting code - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

This page needs to be updated to go into more detail on the topics it contains such as examples and common or known issues. Also include tooling that can help with some of these topics.

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Pre Migration --> Asses the portability of your project --> Visual Basic Considerations - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Per Kathleen Dollard's request we need to address VB.

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Pre-Migration --> Asses the portability of your project --> Overview - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

  • The need to have proper expectations.
  • Analyze your project before starting or committing.

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> Case Studies - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Gather Case Studies from both 1st Parties and 3d Parties and link to them from here.

Some Case studies have already been made public and should be easy such as...

  • https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/azure-active-directorys-gateway-service-is-on-net-core-3-1/
  • https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/migration-of-bings-workflow-engine-to-net-5/

Others may need to be reviewed for public consumption...

  • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/10957/POP3-Service-An-Exchange-service-performance-case-study

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> Recommended Blogs - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Many great blogs have been written by various .NET SMEs on topics that are directly applicable to porting a .NET Framework app to .NET.

Begin this page with a collection of top blogs organized in some kind of logical manner.

Examples at: https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/10208/NET-Core-Adoption-Blogs

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> Reasons for Porting to .NET - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Top reasons for porting from .NET Framework to .NET

  • Performance
    • This is a top attention getter, address the following...
      • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/24103/Perf-Benchmarking
      • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/10521/1st-Party-Provided-Perf-Data
      • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/10893/NET-Core-Perf-Improvements
      • Case Studies...
        • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/10893/NET-Core-Perf-Improvements
        • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/20624/AAD-Gateway-Service-Migration-to-.NET-Core
        • https://devdiv.visualstudio.com/FirstPartyEngagements/_wiki/wikis/FirstPartyEngagements.wiki/11493/Bing-XAP's-Migration-to-.NET-Core
  • Cross-platform
  • Microservice architecture
  • Deployment flexibility
  • Dependency injection

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> Windows to Cross Platform - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

  • Overview of what it meant to decouple the platform from Windows.
  • Address pertinent info, side-effects...
    • Windows Compatibility Pack
    • Known platform issues
    • Known remaining Windows dependencies.

item.title: Issue: Document workload advertising manifest downloads - @tdykstra
item.description:
• Add to the dotnet workload install article an H3 section in the Description H2 explaining advertising manifest updates and options for limiting them
  • For commands that trigger the workload manifest update, add to their CLI reference articles a sentence mentioning advertising manifests, with a link to the h3 in dotnet workload install.
  • Add the --advertising-manifests-only option to the workload update CLI ref article
  • Add the new environment variables to the environment variables reference.

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 2bbfaa61-ebac-d118-6dd1-152a53f37a91
  • Version Independent ID: 55bbba16-ae98-5abc-cd42-1b677105fd29
  • Content: dotnet workload install command - .NET CLI
  • Content Source: docs/core/tools/dotnet-workload-install.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • Technology: dotnet-cli
  • GitHub Login: @tdykstra
  • Microsoft Alias: tdykstra

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> .NET Releases & Servicing Story - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Basically this... https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/net-core-releases-and-support/

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> Breaking Changes - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Rename this page to: "Compatibility and Breaking Changes"

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> .NET Overview - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

Overview of .NET

  • Architecture
  • Separate Runtimes (.net runtime/asp.net runtime/framework runtime)
  • Other app level components WPF, WinForms, etc...

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> General Information --> .NET Framework, .NET Standard & .NET Core - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

  • [ ] Fully explain the relationship between the two platforms and the role .NET Standard plays.
  • [ ] Provide actual scenarios

Top of mind topics to address...

  • Why do we recommend .NET Fx 4.7.2 or greater, what exactly does "there are several issues with consuming .NET Standard libraries that were built for those versions from .NET Framework 4.6.1 projects" mean?

item.title: Issue: Migration Guide --> Overview - @StephenBonikowsky
item.description:
Migration Guide Project New TOC: #23215 *Leverage existing documentation if it exists elsewhere.

The existing page needs to be completely re-written to reflect the new TOC https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/porting/

Currently the page is a somewhat random overview of porting from .NET Framework to .NET with a disconnect collection of topics. These topics are valid but not in this page.

This page should be an overview of the Migration Guide itself and not go into specific topics.

item.title: Issue: What? - @Andy-S-T
item.description: What?

item.title: Issue: Recommendation to use WCF as an alternative to Remoting, when WCF is also removed from .NET 5 - @JasonJShuler
item.description:
WCF is suggested as an alternative to .NET Remoting, but WCF is removed from .NET 5. Shouldn't gRPC be the recommended replacement?

Also, isn't remoting used for Office interop? I haven't looked yet, but is Office interop still available with .NET 5?

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 07cc0e71-91bb-defc-ca58-0023e4d3b276
  • Version Independent ID: f2eb4ab4-72a4-ae0a-acf8-be39bb3f08f7
  • Content: Breaking change: Remoting APIs are obsolete - .NET
  • Content Source: docs/core/compatibility/core-libraries/5.0/remoting-apis-obsolete.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @gewarren
  • Microsoft Alias: gewarren

item.title: Issue: Variant delegates in variant interfaces. Why are keywords needed? - @NikVladi
item.description:
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/481118/variant-delegates-in-variant-interfaces-why-are-ke.html

I really ask you to answer my question as concretely as possible, because I'm just desperate, everyone on the forums behaves as if they understand everything, but everyone just wants to get rid of me, I just don't understand one point and ask the question. The question arose about the need for keywords in variant delegates if their events are in variant interfaces. The question only concerns the in and out keywords for delegates.

Once we create an interface with the in keyword, we need a delegate with the out keyword, why? After all, delagate without the out keyword somehow has covariance, but only for methods, not delegate instances, which means I could have put the method on the event anyway, but not the delegate instance. But we cannot do this.

namespace ConsoleApp1
 {
     class Program
     {
         static void Main (string [] args)
         {
             //Step 1
             B MyMethodReturnB ()
             {
                 return new B ();
             }
    
             // Step 2
             MyClass <A> MyClassForA = new MyClass <A> ();
             MyClassForA.MyEvent + = MyMethodReturnB; // In MyClass <A> (), you can put data of type B using the MyMethodReturnB method and without the out keyword in the MyDelegateReturn delegate
    
             // Step 3
             MyDelegateReturn <B> MyDelegateReturnForB = new MyDelegateReturn <B> (MyMethodReturnB);
             //MyClassForA.MyEvent + = MyDelegateReturnForB; // But when we try to pass data of type B through a delegate variable without the out keyword, an error occurs, this is how variability works in delegates.
                                                            // Without the in and out keywords, one delegate cannot be assigned to another delegate
    
             // Step 4
             IMyInterface <B> IMyInterfaceForB = MyClassForA; // Through the IMyInterface <B> interface without the in keyword, you cannot put MyClass <A> (), but if IMyInterface <B> with the in keyword, then we can
    
             // Step 5
             // Once we create an interface with the in keyword, we need a delegate with the out keyword, why?
             // In the end, delagate without the out keyword somehow has covariance, but only for methods, not delegate instances, which means I could have put the method on the event anyway, but not the delegate instance. But we can't do this
         }
     }
    
     class A
     {
    
     }
    
     class B: A
     {
    
     }
    
     delegate T MyDelegateReturn </ * out * / T> (); // Everything works with the out keyword
    
     interface IMyInterface <in T>
     {
         event MyDelegateReturn <T> MyEvent; // Error due to delegate without out keyword
     }
    
     class MyClass <T>: IMyInterface <T>
     {
         public event MyDelegateReturn <T> MyEvent;
     }
 }

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 0e34cc1a-4658-d7ce-741d-8d81c1701682
  • Version Independent ID: 1fcbc3ff-e3f1-e0a7-7373-16ae150a0de8
  • Content: Variance in Delegates (C#)
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/programming-guide/concepts/covariance-contravariance/variance-in-delegates.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-advanced-concepts
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Auto-label for source and binary incompatibility - @gewarren
item.description: I created two new labels "source incompatible" and "binary incompatible" to be applied to breaking change issues. The breaking change template contains checkboxes to mark the change as either "source incompatible" or "binary incompatible" (PR for that change is #25225). Can the bot please add the corresponding label to these issues?

item.title: Issue: Reorganize CLI TOC - @tdykstra
item.description:
• Reduce The CLI ref TOC from 4 levels of hierarchy to 3 by removing the Reference node under CLI; CLI ref articles will go directly under the CLI node.
  • Move articles that are about the SDK, not only about the CLI, from the CLI node to the SDK node.
  • Move the environment variables reference from dotnet.md to a separate article under the SDK node to make this content more discoverable.

Document Details

item.title: Issue: Web platform installer link gives error saying package doesn't exist - @pchung13
item.description:
[Enter feedback here] The web platform installer link shows an error stating that it cannot find the product you tried to install:

Has the package been deleted?

item.title: Issue: Document `OptimizeImplicitlyTriggeredBuild` property - @Youssef1313
item.description:
This was added in https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/pull/54143 for dev17 and enhanced in https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/pull/54913.

For builds which are indirectly triggered inside Visual Studio from commands such as from 'Run Tests' or 'Start Debugging', we implicitly skip analyzers and nullable analysis to speed up the build. We only do so by default when 'TreatWarningsAsErrors' is not 'true'. For the scenario where 'TreatWarningsAsErrors' is 'true', users can explicitly enable this functionality by setting 'OptimizeImplicitlyTriggeredBuild' to 'true'.

cc @mavasani @gewarren

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 59d4dafc-2217-9a7b-4590-27faa85164cd
  • Version Independent ID: 64b80e80-1c15-c293-466a-918de98e96f6
  • Content: MSBuild properties for Microsoft.NET.Sdk - .NET
  • Content Source: docs/core/project-sdk/msbuild-props.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @gewarren
  • Microsoft Alias: gewarren

item.title: Issue: Conditional post-conditions: `NotNullWhen`, `MaybeNullWhen`, and `NotNullIfNotNull` – `reference type` `nullable annotation` is not part of `method signature` - @Uzivatel919
item.description:
From Conditional post-conditions: NotNullWhen, MaybeNullWhen, and NotNullIfNotNull

When you update the signature for nullable reference types, make message a string? and add an attribute:

Annotation by definition is not part of signature declaration. Beside terminology it works like this also in real.

#nullable enable
class SignatureTestClass
{
  void SignatureTestMethod ( object? obj ) { }
  void SignatureTestMethod ( object obj ) { } // Error CS0111 Type 'SignatureTestClass' already defines a member called 'SignatureTestMethod' with the same parameter types
}
#nullable disable

Sample shows that nullable annotated reference type is not recognized as method overload; it is not recognized as different signature.

Same applies to attributes.

bool SignatureTestMethod2 ( object obj ) => default;
bool SignatureTestMethod2 ( [NotNullWhen(true)] object obj ) => default; // Error CS0111 Type 'SignatureTestClass' already defines a member called 'SignatureTestMethod2' with the same parameter types

It is method declaration what is subject to update.

Other occurences

  • If the url argument isn't null, the output isn't null. Once nullable references are enabled, that signature works correctly, provided your API never accepts a null argument. However, if the argument could be null, then return value could also be null. You could change the signature to the following code:

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 9ae1afa1-7c5f-862b-08f9-23cf5928953e
  • Version Independent ID: 9ae2922b-5108-ed50-768b-30f08cc3fc30
  • Content: C# Reserved attributes: Nullable static analysis
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/language-reference/attributes/nullable-analysis.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-language-reference
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Conditional post-conditions: `NotNullWhen`, `MaybeNullWhen`, and `NotNullIfNotNull` – TryGetMessage sample is wrong - @Uzivatel919
item.description:
From Conditional post-conditions: NotNullWhen, MaybeNullWhen, and NotNullIfNotNull

bool TryGetMessage(string key, out string message)
{
  message = "";
  return true;
}

If the method returns true, the value of message isn't null; otherwise, the method sets message to null.

This example repeats same misconception as described in #25069, i.e. message parameter declared as out string message cannot be set to null in #nullable enable context.

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 9ae1afa1-7c5f-862b-08f9-23cf5928953e
  • Version Independent ID: 9ae2922b-5108-ed50-768b-30f08cc3fc30
  • Content: C# Reserved attributes: Nullable static analysis
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/language-reference/attributes/nullable-analysis.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-language-reference
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Postconditions: `MaybeNull` and `NotNull` – `MaybeNull` usage example has little sense - @Uzivatel919
item.description:
From Postconditions: MaybeNull and NotNull

[return: MaybeNull]
public T Find<T>(IEnumerable<T> sequence, Func<T, bool> predicate)

The preceding code informs callers that the contract implies a non-nullable type, but the return value may actually be null. Use the MaybeNull attribute when your API should be a non-nullable type, typically a generic type parameter, but there may be instances where null would be returned.

This example implies nothing about nullability.

#nullable enable      
public T Find<T> ( IEnumerable<T> sequence, Func<T, bool> predicate ) => default;

void Seek ()
{
  object? x = Find<object?> (Array.Empty<object?> (), _ => true);
  object x1 = Find<object> (Array.Empty<object> (), _ => true);
  int? x2 = Find<int?> (Array.Empty<int?> (), _ => true);
  int x3 = Find<int> (Array.Empty<int> (), _ => true);
}  
#nullable disable

Examples show that any kind of nullability works with Find<T> method.

In fact, from usage perspective for generics there is no difference between T? return type and [return: MaybeNull]. (There are different metadata.). This applies (also) to any reference type. That, since ? in generic method declaration is understood as reference type nullable annotation.

[return: MaybeNull] public T Find<T> ( ) => default;
public T? Find2<T> () => default;    
public T Find3<T> ( ) => default;  // Warning	CS8603	Possible null reference return.

void Test ()
{
  object x = Find<object> (); // Warning	CS8600	Converting null literal or possible null value to non-nullable type.
  object x2 = Find2<object> (); // Warning	CS8600	Converting null literal or possible null value to non-nullable type.
  object x3 = Find3<object> ();
}

Maybe this should not be much about method declaration but rather about its implementation. I guess that paragraph is about to recommend [return: MaybeNull] in order to avoid confusion around T? as return type.

public T ReturnDefault1<T> () => default; // Warning	CS8603	Possible null reference return.
[return: MaybeNull] public T ReturnDefault2<T> () => default; // Ok

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 9ae1afa1-7c5f-862b-08f9-23cf5928953e
  • Version Independent ID: 9ae2922b-5108-ed50-768b-30f08cc3fc30
  • Content: C# Reserved attributes: Nullable static analysis
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/language-reference/attributes/nullable-analysis.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-language-reference
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Postconditions: `MaybeNull` and `NotNull` – `T?` is valid parameter and return type not otherwise - @Uzivatel919
item.description:
From Postconditions: MaybeNull and NotNull

You can't specify that the return value is T?. The method returns null when the sought item isn't found. Since you can't declare a T? return type, you add the MaybeNull annotation to the method return:

(Once again) T? can be used in generic method declaration but it will interpreted/compiled as reference type nullable annotation.

T? Method<T> ( T? t )
{
  object? x   = Method<object> (null);
  object? x1  = Method<object?> (null);
  int? x2     = Method<int?> (null);
  //int? x3     = Method<int> (null); Error  CS1503  Argument 1: cannot convert from '<null>' to 'int'
  int x4     = Method<int> (3);
  
  return default;
}

Example show that for reference type T? declaration is understood. Compiler obviously choses one of available options [for some reason, maybe statistics(?)].

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 9ae1afa1-7c5f-862b-08f9-23cf5928953e
  • Version Independent ID: 9ae2922b-5108-ed50-768b-30f08cc3fc30
  • Content: C# Reserved attributes: Nullable static analysis
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/language-reference/attributes/nullable-analysis.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-language-reference
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Guidance for MatchLambda as argument - @nojaf
item.description:
Hello, in the section Formatting function parameter application it is mentioned that

Parameters should generally be indented relative to the function or fun/function keyword, regardless of the context in which the

However, there is no example given for function | Some _ -> x | None -> y expressions. Consider the following code:

module Foo =
    let bar =
        []
        |> List.choose 
            (function
             | _ -> "")

Would it be formatted like this or follow the rule of Formatting constructors, static members, and member invocations?

Something like

module Foo =
    let bar =
        []
        |> List.choose (
            function
            | _ -> ""
        )

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 8c09996f-24ac-cf39-ef43-d7909cbc2b89
  • Version Independent ID: 1efdd523-8552-6421-e149-74f76826ef8f
  • Content: F# code formatting guidelines
  • Content Source: docs/fsharp/style-guide/formatting.md
  • Product: dotnet-fsharp
  • GitHub Login: @cartermp
  • Microsoft Alias: dotnetcontent

item.title: Issue: Link error : "Let's Learn .NET: F# On July 30, ..." - @fffsharp
item.description:
The link that appears in the header section is broken.
Could you please fix it to the correct link?

item.title: Issue: Document code-analysis GitHub action - @gewarren
item.description:
Add an article about official dotnet (first-party) GitHub actions here. Document the following actions:

  • [ ] dotnet/code-analysis - For this one, also add a backlink from https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/fundamentals/code-analysis/overview.

item.title: Issue: How-To Tag / Label images for object detection - @luisquintanilla
item.description:
Create a new how-to article to show how to label images for object detection using VOTT.

  • Use data prep instructions from model builder detection tutorial for reference
  • Place the doc in the "How-to guides > Model Builder & CLI" section.
  • Create object detection section in load training data into Model Builder as part of #24835 updates
    • The object detection section should include the steps in the model builder object detection tutorial load data section especially the call out to using the VOTT generated json file.
    • Cross-link to new how-to guide on labeling data for object detection

item.title: Issue: Typo in minor roll-forward example - @hvdbrand
item.description:
"When run, version 5.0.* isn't installed, however, 5.1.0 is. Version 3.1.0 will be used." should have been When run, version 5.0.* isn't installed, however, 5.1.0 is. Version 5.1.0 will be used."

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: ab345482-5752-6845-feee-52f0f06c620c
  • Version Independent ID: 3613d4c0-be8e-c5a4-42f4-bec78ab99c75

  • Content Source: docs/core/versions/selection.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @adegeo
  • Microsoft Alias: adegeo

item.title: Issue: Versioninh explanation is inconsistent with the versioning table - @hvdbrand
item.description:
The table and the explanation are a bit confusing. Looking at the table I had the idea that SDK minor (which is multiplied by 100) is related to the runtime patch level. That is not the case.

I actually was expecting the initial version of the SDK to be 5.0.0 instead of 5.0.100. I got most confused by the last line in which and SDK feature change also bumped the runtime version. I would expect that a runtime version of 5.0.0 with and SDK version of 5.0.200 is also allowed. I think that would be clearer.

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 4723eb4d-c4b9-f44c-7753-caa4a3c8cfdd
  • Version Independent ID: 17270776-9bf5-fe60-f9a7-43311e5b4654
  • Content: How the .NET Runtime and SDK are versioned - .NET
  • Content Source: docs/core/versions/index.md
  • Product: dotnet-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.title: Issue: Did we get back in time? - @bstoinev
item.description:
Why the underscore is again the recommended naming convention for private fields? What's wrong with PascalCase for module variables of any kind and access level? And why does access level matter for naming stuff? Don't we use the underscore already for a very specific purpose in C# - namely the discard operator? How does prefixing every single private field with an underscore make the code more readable? On the contrary! This just forces developers into typing stuff like private ContractingPartyModel _contractingPartyModel instead of paying attention to what are they doing and name the fields after the business logic/meaning of the system they author - e.g., private ContractingPartyModel Customer

Next, why the examples are making use of the this keyword? Is this the recommended approach again?!

Document Details

⚠ Do not edit this section. It is required for docs.microsoft.com ➟ GitHub issue linking.

  • ID: 40f42506-f4a5-859a-0266-ded002056d83
  • Version Independent ID: f940f2ad-e13d-4d37-6439-f8afcbc4e4c7
  • Content: C# Coding Conventions
  • Content Source: docs/csharp/fundamentals/coding-style/coding-conventions.md
  • Product: dotnet-csharp
  • Technology: csharp-fundamentals
  • GitHub Login: @BillWagner
  • Microsoft Alias: wiwagn

item.description:

? ... <- ... Used as an operator for setting dynamic properties. You must provide your own implementation.

Examples maybe? Dedicated article?

// Loop until the worker thread activates.
while (!workerThread.IsAlive)
    ;
